blocks.text: Chapter 8.
Rethinking Work and Life: Learning Cultures and Checkups
The ideas in 30 seconds

Organizations built as performance cultures optimize for flawless execution and results, which quietly punishes doubt and dissent; learning cultures treat questioning routines as part of the job, and only they rethink reliably.

Amy Edmondson's research shows that psychological safety — a climate where people can voice mistakes, doubts, and half-formed ideas without fear — is what separates learning teams from silent ones: the best hospital teams didn't make more medication errors, they reported more of them.

Pairing psychological safety with process accountability — judging decisions by the quality of the thinking behind them, not just the outcome — creates the conditions for smart experiments instead of anxious cover-ups.

Grit has a dark side: escalation of commitment means persistent, passionate people are the most likely to keep pouring effort into failing plans, and identity foreclosure locks them into career selves they chose before they knew who they were.
A twice-yearly career checkup — scheduled questions about learning plateaus, changed values, and possible pivots — catches drift early, the way a dental cleaning catches decay.
Lasting success comes not from chasing happiness or "finding" a passion but from developing interest, crafting meaning into work, and holding your plans and identity with psychological flexibility.
How a Performance Culture Destroyed Columbia

In 2003 the space shuttle Columbia disintegrated on re-entry, killing all seven astronauts. During launch, a chunk of foam insulation had broken off and struck the left wing. Engineers noticed and worried; one, Rodney Rocha, pushed for satellite photos of the wing. Managers declined the request, and in the key mission meeting the presumption ran the wrong way — foam strikes had happened on prior flights without catastrophe, so they were treated as a routine maintenance issue rather than a threat to be disproven.

Grant's diagnosis: NASA had drifted from a learning culture into a performance culture. After scores of successful launches, results became the proof of soundness. In performance cultures, people attach their worth to flawless execution, so raising doubts feels like self-incrimination, and asking for more data reads as weakness. The same dynamic had contributed to the Challenger explosion seventeen years earlier; the lesson hadn't stuck because the culture, not just the procedure, was the problem.

The organization also suffered from what sociologists call normalization of deviance: anomalies that don't immediately cause disaster get reclassified as normal. Each foam strike that didn't kill anyone became evidence that foam strikes were safe — outcome data substituting for actual analysis.

Astronaut Ellen Ochoa, the first Latina in space and later director of NASA's Johnson Space Center, embodied the alternative. She carried a card of questions to ask in critical situations — variations on "How do you know?" and "What leads you to that conclusion?" After Columbia, as a senior leader she worked to make that kind of questioning standard, so that expressing uncertainty became a contribution rather than a confession. In a learning culture, confidence attaches to the organization's ability to find and fix errors, not to the claim that errors don't exist.
Why the Best Teams Report More Errors

In the mid-1990s, Harvard's Amy Edmondson studied medication errors in hospital units, expecting the best-led, most cohesive teams to make the fewest mistakes. The data showed the opposite: the strongest teams reported more errors. Digging in, she found they weren't making more mistakes — they were the only ones willing to admit them. Weaker teams hid errors, so nothing was learned. This discovery launched the research program on psychological safety: a climate of trust and respect in which people believe they can take interpersonal risks — ask naive questions, flag problems, admit failures — without being punished or humiliated.

The finding has replicated widely; Google's Project Aristotle later identified psychological safety as the single strongest predictor of team effectiveness. But the concept is routinely misunderstood. Psychological safety is not niceness, comfort, or lowered standards. It is not freedom from consequences. It is the assurance that candor itself won't be penalized, which is precisely what makes high standards achievable — problems surface while they're still fixable.

Leaders can't create it by announcement. Grant, with Constantinos Coutifaris, ran a field experiment on how managers might build it. Managers who merely solicited feedback saw only a short-lived bump — if they didn't act on it, people concluded speaking up was pointless. Managers who shared criticism they had received about themselves produced psychological safety that was still measurable a year later. Voluntarily exposing your own flaws proves, rather than promises, that imperfection is discussable. At the Gates Foundation, Melinda Gates modeled this by having leaders — herself included — read harsh employee criticism aloud on video and by openly discussing her own failures, shifting a culture where employees had felt pressure to appear perfect.
Process Accountability: Judge the Thinking, Not Just the Result

Psychological safety alone isn't sufficient; without accountability it can produce a comfort zone where nothing improves. Edmondson's framework crosses the two: high safety plus high accountability yields the learning zone. The question is what to hold people accountable for.

Most organizations run on outcome accountability: rewards and blame track results. The problem is that outcomes are noisy. A good decision can end badly, and a reckless one can get lucky. Rewarding outcomes alone teaches people to hide risk and repeat lucky gambles. The complement is process accountability: evaluating how a decision was made — what evidence was gathered, what alternatives were considered, what dissent was heard — before the result is known.

The combination matters. Outcome accountability without psychological safety creates an anxiety zone where people conceal problems (NASA before Columbia). Process accountability plus psychological safety lets teams classify a bad outcome from a rigorous process as a smart experiment worth repeating, and a good outcome from a sloppy process as a warning rather than a template.

Two supporting habits follow. First, keep a rethinking scorecard: track the quality of your decision processes separately from their results, so luck doesn't masquerade as skill. Second, be suspicious of the phrase "best practices." Calling a practice "best" implies the search is over and freezes it in place; learning cultures aim instead for better practices, treating every routine as provisional and every process as permanently open to challenge. Ochoa's card of questions is the personal version of this: institutionalized doubt, carried in a pocket.
Grit's Dark Side: Escalation of Commitment and Identity Foreclosure

Chapter 11 turns the lens from organizations to life plans. Grant tells the story of his cousin Ryan, who decided young that he would be a doctor, ground through pre-med, medical school, and a punishing neurosurgery residency — nursing doubts for years but never seriously revisiting the decision, because so much had already been invested and so many people knew him as "the future surgeon."

Two forces drive this trap. Escalation of commitment, documented by Barry Staw, is our tendency to double down on a failing course of action. Sunk costs play a role, but the deeper drivers are ego and image: quitting feels like admitting the earlier choice — and the self who made it — was wrong. Research shows that grit amplifies the risk: grittier people persist longer at unwinnable tasks and are more prone to escalate when a venture is failing. Perseverance is a virtue only when the goal still deserves it; applied to a dead end, grit becomes the engine of the dead end.

The second force is identity foreclosure: settling on a self-definition prematurely, before exploring alternatives — a concept from developmental psychology. Grant argues the question "What do you want to be when you grow up?" invites foreclosure by teaching children that a self is a single occupation to be picked early and defended forever. Careers are decisions to keep making, not identities to protect. When work becomes who you are rather than what you currently do, every doubt about the job becomes a threat to the self — exactly the totalitarian-ego dynamic that blocks rethinking (see Chapter 3).
The escape is the same move recommended for beliefs: detach your identity from your current plan. You are not your career; you are the values you want your career to serve.
The Twice-Yearly Career Checkup

We go to the doctor and dentist on a schedule even when nothing hurts, but most people reexamine their careers only when misery forces the issue — by which point escalation has years of momentum. Grant's prescription is a career checkup: twice a year, put an appointment on the calendar to interrogate your work life while the stakes are still low.

The questions to ask: When did I form the aspirations I'm currently pursuing, and how have I changed since then? Have I hit a learning plateau — am I still growing, or just repeating? Do my daily tasks still connect to my values? If I were starting fresh today, would I choose this path? Is it time to consider a pivot, a stretch role, or a deliberate experiment in a new direction?

The point isn't to quit every eighteen months. Most checkups end with recommitment — but now it's a chosen commitment rather than an inherited one. And when the answers trend bad across consecutive checkups, you catch the drift years earlier than crisis would have forced you to. Grant treats the checkup as the personal equivalent of a learning culture: a standing, scheduled invitation to doubt yourself constructively, so that rethinking doesn't depend on pain.
Redefine Success: Meaning and Flexibility Over Passion-Chasing

The epilogue and the close of chapter 11 attack two pieces of standard life advice. First, "pursue happiness." Research by Iris Mauss shows that the more people explicitly value and chase happiness, the less happy they tend to become — constant evaluation of your feelings crowds out actually experiencing your life. Happiness also follows meaning more durably than it follows pleasure: purpose sustains well-being after the fun fades.

Second, "follow your passion." Paul O'Keefe's research on theories of interest finds that people who believe passions are found give up when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed deepen them through effort. Passion is grown, not discovered. And meaning can be built into a job you already have: Amy Wrzesniewski's work on job crafting shows people can redesign their tasks and relationships around their values — like the custodian at a Michigan cancer center who defined her role not as cleaning but as protecting fragile patients and comforting their families, and did her job accordingly.

Grant closes by practicing what he preaches: he rethought and rewrote the book's own ending, showing his discarded drafts. His final redefinition of success rests on psychological flexibility — holding plans in pencil, identities loosely, and values firmly. Define yourself by what you care about and how you think, not by what you currently believe or do, and both your work and your life stay open to revision. That openness, not any fixed achievement, is the payoff of thinking again.
In practice

Schedule two career checkups on your calendar right now, six months apart; at each, ask whether you've hit a learning plateau, whether your work still matches your values, and whether you'd choose this path if starting fresh today.

In your next team meeting, share a piece of critical feedback you've received about yourself before asking others for candor — modeled vulnerability builds psychological safety; merely requesting feedback does not.
Review your last major decision by process, not outcome: write down what evidence you gathered and what alternatives you weighed, and score the thinking separately from the result.
Replace one "best practice" in your workflow with a standing question — "How do we know this is still the best way?" — and assign someone to challenge it quarterly.
When you notice yourself persisting mainly because of what you've already invested or how quitting would look, name it as escalation of commitment and re-decide from today's information only.
Instead of hunting for a ready-made passion, pick one current responsibility and craft it toward your values — redesign the task, the relationships, or its purpose until it carries meaning.
